segments.caption: The video segment begins with a static title slide titled 'Lecanemab in Early Alzheimer’s Disease' on a deep blue gradient background, with citation text at the bottom reading 'C.H. van Dyck., et al., New England Journal of Medicine. January 5, 2023.' The slide then transitions to display three bullet points: '1795 early Alzheimer’s participants,' '898 lecanemab, 897 placebo,' and '18 month study,' providing key details about the clinical trial. The scene then shifts to a black background with a line graph titled 'ADAS-Cog14 Score,' showing the 'Adjusted Mean Change from Baseline' on the y-axis (with an arrow indicating 'Worsening' downward) and 'Visit (month)' on the x-axis from 0 to 18. Initially, only a blue line labeled 'Placebo' is shown, sloping downward to indicate cognitive decline over time. A yellow line labeled 'Lecanemab' then appears, positioned above the placebo line, indicating a slower rate of decline. The graph visually demonstrates that lecanemab significantly slows cognitive deterioration compared to placebo, with a statistical note 'P<0.001 at 18 mo' appearing briefly to emphasize the significance of the difference. The citation remains at the bottom throughout.

The slide evolves to include bullet points about the study design and then transitions to a black-background line graph comparing placebo and lecanemab outcomes on the ADAS-Cog14 score over 18 months, with a downward arrow indicating worsening cognitive function.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a presentation slide from the New England Journal of Medicine, displaying a line graph titled 'C ADAS-Cog14 Score' that compares the cognitive decline over 18 months between participants receiving Lecanemab (orange line) and a placebo (blue line). The y-axis, labeled 'Adjusted Mean Change from Baseline,' shows worsening cognitive function as values increase downward, indicated by a downward arrow. The graph demonstrates that the Lecanemab group experiences a slower rate of cognitive decline compared to the placebo group, with a statistically significant difference noted at 18 months (P<0.001). The slide includes the citation 'C.H. van Dyck., et al., New England Journal of Medicine. January 5, 2023.' The graph is then zoomed in to highlight the data points at 15 and 18 months, emphasizing the divergence between the two groups. The scene transitions to a bar chart titled 'Amyloid-Related Imaging Abnormalities with Edema or Effusions,' showing the percentage of participants experiencing these side effects: 12.6% (113/898) in the Lecanemab group versus 1.7% (15/897) in the placebo group. The same citation remains at the bottom of the slide. The visual presentation is clean, with a dark blue gradient background and white text, focusing on the data without any additional animations or speaker presence.

April, 2023' in the bottom right corner. The slide lists two sub-classes of anti-β-amyloid drugs: 'Secretase inhibitors' and 'Anti-amyloid monoclonal antibodies.' A light blue rectangular box then appears below the second bullet point, listing specific drugs: 'aducanamab - Aduhelm™' and 'lecanemab - Leqembi™.' The slide transitions to a new layout, presenting bullet points about the study's methodology: 'Systematic review of patients enrolled in randomized trials of anti-Aβ drugs (n=8062 to 10279)' and 'Detailed evaluation of volumetric MRI data involving hippocampus, lateral ventricals and whole brain.' The next frame returns to the drug classification slide, now with a blue arrow pointing from 'Secretase inhibitors' to 'hippocampal and whole brain atrophy,' followed by another arrow from 'Anti-amyloid monoclonal antibodies' to 'ventricular enlargement,' visually linking drug classes to their associated brain structural changes. The visual narrative emphasizes the study's focus on brain volume loss and its association with specific anti-amyloid therapies, using clear text and directional arrows to convey causal relationships.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a dark blue gradient slide titled 'Anti-β-Amyloid Drugs,' listing two drug classes: 'Secretase inhibitors' and 'Anti-amyloid monoclonal antibodies.' A light blue box below the second bullet point lists 'aducanamab - Aduhelm™' and 'lecanemab - Leqembi™.' Blue arrows are added to link 'Secretase inhibitors' to 'hippocampal and whole brain atrophy' and 'Anti-amyloid monoclonal antibodies' to 'ventricular enlargement,' with the citation 'Alves, F., et al., Neurology. April, 2023' at the bottom right. The scene transitions to a new slide titled 'Accelerated Brain Volume Loss Caused by Anti-β-Amyloid Drugs: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis,' citing JAMA, January 8, 2020. It states that mild cognitively impaired participants treated with anti-Aβ drugs regress toward Alzheimer’s dementia brain volumes ~8 months earlier than untreated individuals, suggesting these therapies may accelerate brain atrophy. The next frame shows a screenshot of a MedPage Today article titled 'Brains Shrink With Anti-Amyloid Alzheimer's Drugs,' dated March 31, 2023, with an illustration of neurons and amyloid plaques. This is followed by a screenshot of a Neurology journal editorial titled 'Untangling Eligibility: Real-World Application of Anti-Beta Amyloid Monoclonal Antibodies,' dated August 16, 2023, authored by Matthew D Howe, Gil D. Rabinovici, and Stephen P Salloway. The final frame is a plain dark blue slide with the same title and citation, then text appears stating 'Fewer than 10% of early Alzheimer’s patients in real-world cohort would qualify for anti-amyloid beta monoclonal antibodies.' The visual narrative progresses from drug classification and effects to meta-analytic findings, media coverage, and real-world eligibility challenges, maintaining a clinical and academic tone throughout.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a slide titled 'Two Phase 3 Trials of Gantenerumab in Early Alzheimer’s Disease,' featuring a diagram illustrating a vial labeled 'Gantenerumab' with a syringe, purple Y-shaped monoclonal antibodies, and a brain cross-section with amyloid plaques being targeted. The citation 'Bateman, R.J., et al. New England Journal of Medicine. November 16, 2023' is displayed at the bottom right. The slide transitions to a bullet-point list detailing the trial parameters: 985 and 980 early Alzheimer’s patients over 116 weeks, aged 50–90 (average 72), with measurements of amyloid burden and cognitive function at 116 weeks. Next, a line graph titled 'Amyloid Level on PET' appears, showing the adjusted mean change from baseline (in centiloids) over time (0, 52, and 116 weeks). A red line labeled 'placebo' remains near zero, while a green line labeled 'gantenerumab' steeply declines to approximately -60 centiloids by week 116. The final slide presents a bar chart titled 'Change in Clinical Dementia Rating (Scale-Sum of Boxes Score)' for Trial 1, with a green bar for 'Gantenerumab' and a red bar for 'Placebo,' both showing positive changes from baseline, though the placebo bar is slightly higher. The citation remains consistent throughout.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a bar chart titled 'Change in Clinical Dementia Rating (Scale-Sum of Boxes Score)' on a dark background, showing two trials. In Trial 1, a green bar for 'Gantenerumab' and a red bar for 'Placebo' both indicate positive changes from baseline, with the placebo bar slightly higher. The chart then transitions to include Trial 2, where both bars are again shown, maintaining similar values. The scene shifts to a line graph titled 'ADCS-ADL Total Score' from the 'GRADUATE I' study, plotting 'Adjusted Mean Change from Baseline' over weeks (0 to 116). Two lines, one for 'Gantenerumab (N=497)' and one for 'Placebo (N=479)', both trend downward, indicating worsening, with the placebo line slightly lower. A text box notes the 'Difference at wk 116, 1.11 (95% CI, -0.48 to 2.70)'. The video then transitions to a black screen with the title 'Amyloid-Related Imaging Abnormalities with Edema' and the y-axis labeled 'Percentage of Participants'. A bar chart appears, showing a red bar for 'Placebo' at approximately 3% and a green bar for 'Gantenerumab' at approximately 25%, indicating a significantly higher percentage of participants with imaging abnormalities in the gantenerumab group. The citation 'Bateman, R.J., et al. New England Journal of Medicine. November 16, 2023' is consistently displayed at the bottom right throughout the segment.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a bar chart titled 'Amyloid-Related Imaging Abnormalities with Edema,' displaying a stark contrast between the placebo group (red bar, ~3%) and the gantenerumab group (green bar, ~25%), indicating a significantly higher incidence of imaging abnormalities in the drug group. The citation 'Bateman, R.J., et al. New England Journal of Medicine. November 16, 2023' is visible at the bottom right. The scene transitions to a dark blue slide titled 'Two Phase 3 Trials of Gantenerumab in Early Alzheimer’s Disease,' with the word 'CONCLUSIONS' in red, followed by a quote: 'Among persons with early Alzheimer’s disease, the use of gantenerumab led to a lower amyloid plaque burden than placebo at 116 weeks but was not associated with slower clinical decline.' The same citation remains. Next, a black-and-white image of Albert Einstein appears on the left, with his quote, 'The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results,' displayed in white text on the right. The final scene shows a screenshot of a MedPage Today article titled 'Controversial Alzheimer's Drug Abandoned — Biogen will stop developing and selling aducanumab,' dated January 31, 2024. An image of the Aduhelm (aducanumab-avwa) vial and box is shown below the headline. A text box overlays the image, stating: 'The drugmaker will stop developing and selling aducanumab, an anti-amyloid monoclonal antibody that received FDA accelerated approval in 2021. Biogen also will terminate the confirmatory ENVISION trial, which was a requirement of the drug's accelerated approval.' The background remains dark blue throughout the article segment.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a static screenshot of a MedPage Today article titled 'Controversial Alzheimer's Drug Abandoned,' dated January 31, 2024, authored by Judy George. The article announces that Biogen will cease developing and selling aducanumab, an anti-amyloid monoclonal antibody that received FDA accelerated approval in 2021, and will terminate the confirmatory ENVISION trial. An image of the Aduhelm (aducanumab-avwa) vial and box is displayed below the headline, with a text box overlay reiterating the drug's discontinuation. The scene then transitions to a cover page of the '2023 Alzheimer’s Disease Facts and Figures' special report by the Alzheimer’s Association, featuring a doctor holding a patient’s hand and a collage of healthcare interactions. This is followed by a series of informational slides: first, a 'PREVALENCE' slide stating 'AN ESTIMATED 6.7 MILLION AMERICANS ARE LIVING WITH ALZHEIMER’S DEMENTIA,' accompanied by a photo of an elderly woman; next, a 'MORTALITY AND MORBIDITY' slide noting that 61% of people aged 70 with Alzheimer’s are expected to die before 80, compared to 30% without the disease, with an image of an older man in glasses looking out a window; finally, an 'OVERVIEW' slide explaining that Alzheimer’s begins 20 years or more before memory loss, paired with a photo of an older Black woman. The segment concludes with a detailed flowchart titled 'Patient Journey from Awareness of Cognitive Issues to Care from a Physician Specialist and Treatment,' illustrating the diagnostic and treatment pathway with icons and text for each stage: cognitive concerns, initial PCP visit, initial specialist physician visit, diagnostic testing and imaging, specialist follow-up and treatment discussion, and treatment and ongoing specialist care.

The scene then transitions to a static screenshot of a commentary article from 'Neurology Reviews, November, 2023' by Michael Merzenich, PhD, titled 'Do new Alzheimer’s drugs get us closer to solving the Alzheimer’s disease riddle?'. The article's text is highlighted with key points appearing as bullet points on the right side of the screen. The first bullet point states, '...the trials of these drugs do not support the hypothesis that amyloid is the primary neuropathological agent underlying the progressive neurobehavioral decline in Alzheimer’s disease.' The second bullet point follows, 'The more obvious path to winning the battle against this human scourge is prevention.' The third bullet point appears, stating, '...80-90% of prospective cases can be substantially delayed or prevented.' The background remains a consistent view of the article text throughout, with the bullet points appearing sequentially to emphasize the commentary's main arguments against the efficacy of amyloid-targeting drugs and the importance of prevention.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a dark blue gradient slide titled 'Insulin Resistance and Alzheimer’s Disease: Bioenergetic Linkages,' citing Neth, B., et al., Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience, October 2017. Initially blank, the slide then displays a quote: 'Moreover, we may be able to characterize “brain metabolic fingerprints” that may be used to offer patients and research participants more personalized therapeutic options if they were to develop a condition impacting brain metabolism.' This transitions to a black background showing two grayscale MRI brain scans labeled 'Control' and 'Alzheimer’s Disease,' highlighting structural differences. Next, the view shifts to two colorful FDG-PET scans under the same labels, illustrating metabolic activity with red/yellow indicating higher uptake and green/blue lower, showing reduced activity in the Alzheimer’s brain. The segment then presents a bar chart titled 'Brain Glucose Uptake' with a yellow bar for 'controls' (around 28 µmol/100 g/min) and a red bar for 'mild AD' (around 16 µmol/100 g/min), citing Frontiers in Molecular Neuroscience, July 8, 2016. Finally, a second bar chart titled 'Glucose uptake' appears, showing three bars: yellow for 'Older' (0.053), green for 'MCI' (0.050), and red for 'AD' (0.045), all measured in Brain 18 F-FDG uptake constant (min⁻¹), with the same citation. The visual narrative emphasizes the progressive decline in brain glucose metabolism from healthy aging to mild cognitive impairment to Alzheimer’s disease, using both imaging and quantitative data to illustrate the bioenergetic linkages between insulin resistance and Alzheimer’s.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a dark blue gradient background displaying the title of a BMJ study: 'Association between healthy lifestyle and memory decline in older adults: 10 year, population based, prospective cohort study,' with the citation 'Jia, J., et al., The BMJ. January, 2023' in the bottom right. The screen then transitions to show a key finding: 'Our results show that a healthy lifestyle was associated with a slower rate of memory decline in cognitively normal older individuals, including in people who are genetically susceptible to memory decline.' Next, a bulleted list appears, detailing the study's parameters: 29,072 participants with normal cognitive function, 46% women, followed for 10 years, with APOE-ε4 status and cognitive evaluations recorded. The scene shifts to a colorful infographic titled 'Comparison,' illustrating six healthy lifestyle factors—healthy diet, regular physical exercise, active social contact, active cognitive activity, never or former smoking, and never drinking—with participants categorized into 'Unfavourable' (0-1 factors, 6967 people), 'Average' (2-3 factors, 16,549 people), and 'Favourable' (4-6 factors, 5556 people). Finally, a bar chart appears, showing the 'Estimated change in memory function over 10 years' for 'Non-carriers of APOE-ε4,' with green (favorable), blue (average), and red (unfavorable) bars indicating progressively greater memory decline, with the favorable group showing the least decline.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a dark blue slide titled 'Exercise restores brain insulin sensitivity in sedentary adults who are overweight and obese,' citing Kullmann, S., et al., JCI Insight, September 2022. The slide then transitions to display bullet points detailing the study: 14 women and 7 men aged 21–59 years, BMI range 27.5–45.5 kg/m², fMRI scans before and after nasal insulin administration, and an 8-week supervised aerobic program. Next, a diagram appears illustrating the experimental timeline: fMRI 1, followed by nasal insulin administration, then a 30-minute interval, and finally fMRI 2. The scene shifts to a brain scan image labeled 'Δ CBF Putamen (mL/100g/min) (comparing before and after insulin),' showing a crosshair in the putamen region with a color scale from T=3 (red) to T=5 (yellow). Finally, a box plot appears on the right side of the screen, labeled 'Comparison group (no exercise),' displaying data points for 'pre' and 'post-8-wk' measurements, with a red line indicating the median in each group.

segments.caption: The video begins with a static screenshot of a Forbes article titled 'Erectile Dysfunction Drugs Like Viagra May Reduce Alzheimer’s Risk, Study Finds,' dated February 7, 2024. The article, authored by Arianna Johnson, features an image of an elderly person’s hands interacting with a colorful cognitive test board, symbolizing cognitive function. The topline summary highlights a Wednesday study suggesting these drugs may reduce Alzheimer’s risk. The scene then transitions to a black background displaying two FDG-PET brain scans labeled 'Control' and 'Alzheimer’s Disease,' with text indicating 'Glucose utilization' and 'Vascular perfusion' to emphasize metabolic differences. Next, a blue slide appears with the title 'Impact of Nitric Oxide Bioavailability on the Progressive Cerebral and Peripheral Circulatory Impairments During Aging and Alzheimer’s Disease,' citing Venturelli, M., et al., Frontiers in Physiology, March 2018. The slide then updates to include bullet points detailing the study’s methodology: 88 subjects across young, old, MCI, and AD stages, with measurements of cortical perfusion, ICA blood flow, femoral artery blood flow, systemic vascular perfusion, and nitric oxide bioavailability. The visual narrative progresses from a news headline to scientific imaging and research data, establishing a connection between vascular health, nitric oxide, and Alzheimer’s disease.

The slide lists 88 subjects categorized as young (late 20s), old (late 70s), MCI, and AD (stages 1-3), and outlines measured parameters: cortical perfusion, ICA blood flow, femoral artery blood flow, systemic vascular perfusion, and nitric oxide bioavailability. The scene transitions to a black background displaying 'Cerebral bloodflow maps' with six columns labeled 'young,' 'old,' 'MCI,' 'AD 1,' 'AD 2,' and 'AD 3,' each containing three brain scans in a heat map color scheme (blue to yellow/red) indicating blood flow intensity, showing a progressive reduction in flow with advancing disease. The final slide, titled 'Nitric Oxide Bioavailability,' presents two green bar charts side-by-side: 'Muscle perfusion' and 'Plasma nitrates,' both showing a consistent decline from 'young' to 'AD3,' visually linking reduced nitric oxide bioavailability with aging and Alzheimer’s progression. segments.caption: The video begins with a dark blue slide titled 'Nitric Oxide Bioavailability,' displaying two green bar charts. The left chart, labeled 'Muscle perfusion,' shows a decline from 'young' to 'AD3,' while the right chart, 'Plasma nitrates,' similarly decreases across the same groups. The scene transitions to a text slide with the title 'Impact of Nitric Oxide Bioavailability on the Progressive Cerebral and Peripheral Circulatory Impairments During Aging and Alzheimer’s Disease,' followed by two bullet points: the first states that progressive changes in NO bioavailability and circulation are directly associated with Alzheimer’s disease (AD) and not aging, and the second suggests the link between cardiovascular and central nervous system degeneration in AD patients is likely due to NO depletion. The background remains dark blue throughout. The video then shifts to a white background, where a fresh beetroot with green leaves appears on the left. segments.caption: The video segment begins with a dark blue slide titled 'Over-the-counter mouthwash use and risk of pre-diabetes/diabetes,' listing key study details: 945 overweight/obese adults, nondiabetic, followed for 3 years, with frequency of mouthwash use as the variable. The slide transitions to reveal the finding: compared to nonusers, using mouthwash ≥2 times daily is associated with a 49% increased risk of pre-diabetes or diabetes. The next slide, titled 'Over-the-counter mouthwash use, nitric oxide and hypertension risk,' displays an abstract from a 2020 Blood Pressure study by Joshipura et al., which then transitions to a summary slide with 540 participants, followed for 3 years, and the finding that ≥2 daily uses are associated with a 117% increased risk of hypertension. The text '2 times daily' is highlighted in yellow for emphasis. The scene then shifts to a white background with a diagram illustrating the nitrate-to-nitric oxide pathway: a fresh beetroot on the left, an arrow pointing to an open human mouth, and a flowchart showing 'nitrate → nitrite → Nitric Oxide.' The final slide, titled 'Nitrate-responsive oral microbiome modulates nitric oxide homeostasis and blood pressure in humans,' presents findings from a 2018 study by Vanhatalo et al., stating that nitrate supplementation caused a 225% relative increase in Proteobacteria and a 5 mmHg decrease in systolic BP in 70-79 year olds. All slides maintain a consistent dark blue background with white text and citations at the bottom right.
